title: podman-remote: sometimes shows container status as 'stopped'
body:
A fast sequence of `podman-remote run, stop, inspect` will sometimes show container status as 'stopped' (expected: 'exited'). A subsequent inspect will show `exited` as expected. I thought 'stopped' had been deprecated? 

Reproducer:
```bash
#!/bin/sh

PODMAN=${PODMAN:-./bin/podman-remote}

while :; do
    cid=$(sudo $PODMAN run --name foo -d alpine sleep 60)
    sudo $PODMAN stop foo >/dev/null
    status=$(sudo $PODMAN inspect --format '{{.State.Status}}' foo)
    echo $status
    if [ "$status" = "exited" ]; then
        sudo $PODMAN rm foo >/dev/null
    else
        echo "*** TRIGGERED ***"
        while [ "$status" != "exited" ]; do
            status=$(sudo $PODMAN inspect --format '{{.State.Status}}' foo)
            echo "-- " $status
        done
        echo "-- ok"
        exit 1
    fi
done
```
It has never taken more than five iterations to trigger; usually it's one or two. I cannot get it to trigger using plain `podman` (not remote).

Sample output:
```
exited
exited
exited
stopped
*** TRIGGERED ***
--  exited
-- ok
```

master @ 8d93b72b5511321e394e1490c66cda378bdb1514

comments.body:
`stopped` is still a potential state, however we should be transitioning out of it very quickly - on my local system, cleanup seems to happen around .25-.5 seconds after container exit, so we spend that time in the `stopped` state before transitioning to `exit`. I imagine that cleanup would take longer on a testing VM, so you could be seeing that here - a few seconds delay as cleanup runs and takes the container from stopped to exited.

We mostly deprecated filtering against `stopped` because I thought we'd basically never hit that small timing window. It might be worth adding it back if we spend multiple seconds per container transitioning.
@mheon do you want to add the stopped back in ?

This is a mismatch between `ps` filtering and display. We can fix it, but it's potentially confusing - I don't think our stopped/exited matches Docker, so the intent of filters may be lost.

I'll poke around.

Hm. It looks like we can restore the distinction between `stopped` and `exited` without serious issue for Docker compat, though I am worried that it would regress versus Podman 1.0, where all containers were both 'stopped' and 'exited' when they stopped.
I'm closing this because of risk of regressions vs 1.0... If people feel strongly, we can revisit
